sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An acute dystonic reaction is an extrapyramidal side effect characterised by sudden involuntary abnormal postures or repetitive movements due to dopamine receptor blockade, often after antipsychotics or antiemetics.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Laryngospasm or upper-airway spasm with stridor or dyspnoea
- Oculogyric crisis or abnormal eye deviation
- Torticollis
- Opisthotonus
- Jaw spasm, tongue protrusion, dysarthria, grimacing
- Anxiety, agitation, diaphoresis, tachycardia
- Mental status is usually preserved

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- More common in younger patients and after dopamine-blocking medications such as metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, haloperidol, or risperidone
- Can be mistaken for seizure, meningitis, tetanus, anaphylaxis, or behavioural disturbance
- Medication history and time relation to symptom onset are important
- Airway involvement, stridor, or reduced ventilation require immediate escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position for airway protection and patient comfort
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic or in respiratory distress
- Be prepared to assist ventilations if airway spasm or reduced respiratory effort develops
- Confirm recent exposure to dopamine-blocking medication and exclude anaphylaxis, seizure, or other airway emergencies

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider vascular access if medication therapy or airway deterioration is likely
- Consider IM or IV benztropine (1-2 mg) in line with local protocol or in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider promethazine (25-50 mg IM) if benztropine is unavailable or unsuitable and this is consistent with local protocol
- Consider benzodiazepines for persistent severe spasms or marked distress after senior clinical review
- Escalate urgently if laryngospasm, ongoing respiratory compromise, or diagnostic uncertainty persists

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Common acute dystonic patterns include oculogyric crisis, torticollis, jaw spasm, tongue protrusion, and opisthotonus
- Tardive dyskinesia and chorea are different movement disorders and should not be confused with acute dystonia
